name: Chumphon Pinnacle
description: Koh Tao's premier dive site. A massive granite pinnacle rising from the deep, surrounded by smaller pinnacles. Famous for whale shark sightings and enormous schools of barracuda and batfish. The top is carpeted with pink anemones.

name: Sail Rock
description: A massive underwater rock formation with a chimney (vertical swim-through) cutting through the rock from 5m to 18m. The best dive site in the Gulf of Thailand for pelagic encounters.

name: Japanese Gardens
description: A gently sloping reef between Koh Tao and Koh Nang Yuan. Named for the garden-like arrangement of hard and soft corals. Perfect for beginners and snorkelers with incredible biodiversity in shallow water.

name: Twins
description: Two underwater pinnacles between Koh Tao and Koh Nang Yuan. Excellent coral coverage with a good variety of reef fish. One of the island's most popular sites for discovery dives.

name: White Rock
description: Koh Tao's most-dived site, named for the white bleached rock visible at low tide. Amazing for night diving with active nocturnal creatures. Large scattered boulders create swim-throughs and overhangs.
highlights: Best night diving on Koh Tao

name: Green Rock
description: A series of massive boulders creating an underwater maze of swim-throughs, caves, and tunnels. Named for the green algae that covers the rocks. Home to large moray eels and sea snakes.

name: Shark Island
description: A rocky island off the southeast coast of Koh Tao. Strong currents bring nutrients that attract diverse marine life. Named for its shark-fin shape above water, not for shark sightings.

name: HTMS Sattakut
description: A decommissioned Royal Thai Navy vessel sunk in 2011 to create an artificial reef. The 49-meter ship sits upright on the sandy bottom and has become home to an impressive variety of marine life.

name: Mango Bay
description: A sheltered bay on the north coast of Koh Tao. Calm, clear waters make it ideal for snorkeling and discovery dives. Patch reefs on a sandy bottom with excellent visibility.

name: Aow Leuk
description: A beautiful bay on the southeast coast with a gently sloping reef starting from the beach. Great shore entry dive with a mix of sandy bottom and coral patches.

name: Tanote Bay
description: East coast bay with large granite boulders both above and below water. Easy beach entry with interesting rock formations creating hideouts for marine life.

name: Hin Wong Pinnacle
description: An exposed pinnacle site on the east coast. Deeper and more challenging than nearby sites with occasional strong currents. Rewarded with less-visited reefs and bigger marine life.

name: Pottery
description: Small rocky pinnacles and boulders rising from a sandy bottom just south of Mae Haad pier. Popular for night dives due to proximity to shore and potential for bioluminescence.

name: Three Rocks
description: Three underwater pinnacles with good coral coverage and abundant reef fish. Regularly visited by green and hawksbill sea turtles. Great for macro photography.

name: Hin Pee Wee
description: Three pinnacles on Koh Tao's northwest side near the HTMS Sattakut wreck. Less crowded than other sites, with white-eyed moray eels, blue-spotted rays, and occasional seahorse sightings.

name: 313 Wreck (HTMS Suphairin)
description: A 44.9m Royal Thai Navy attack ship sunk in 2023 as part of an underwater ecosystem rehabilitation project. Located northwest of No Name Pinnacle and rapidly becoming colonized by marine life.

name: No Name Pinnacle
description: A granite pinnacle rising from 30m to about 18m between Koh Tao and Koh Nang Yuan. Combined with the adjacent HTMS Suphairin wreck, it offers encounters with schools of fusiliers, trevally, and large groupers.

name: Red Rock
description: A lone pinnacle off the east coast of Koh Nang Yuan. Famous for its swim-through cavern and regular turtle sightings. Often combined as a drift dive ending at Japanese Gardens.

name: Junkyard Reef
description: An artificial reef created in 2006 by ECO Koh Tao from recycled materials crafted to support marine life. Now a thriving ecosystem with juvenile harlequin sweetlips, anemonefish, and pufferfish.

name: Aow Mao
description: A lesser-visited bay on Koh Tao's east coast with incredibly healthy coral reefs due to minimal human traffic. The name translates to 'Drunken Bay.' Also the location of the 312 wreck nearby.

name: 312 Wreck (HTMS Hanhak Sattru)
description: A 44.9m Royal Thai Navy attack ship sunk in 2023 in Aow Mao bay. The wreck sits upright on the seabed and is rapidly becoming colonized by marine life as part of the underwater ecosystem rehabilitation project.

name: Laem Thian
description: A remote east coast site featuring dramatic underwater topography with caves, swim-throughs, and caverns formed by massive boulders. The 'Boom Boom Room' swim-through creates wave surge sensations.

name: Hin Ngam
description: Three pinnacles on the southeast coast between Aow Leuk and Shark Island. Coral bommies scattered across sandy floor provide homes for frogfish, scorpionfish, and nudibranchs.

name: Southwest Pinnacle
description: A dramatic multi-peaked granite formation about 45 minutes southwest of Koh Tao. Seven large pinnacles in a circular shape, carpeted in corals and anemones. A pelagic hotspot with whale shark sightings during migration season.

name: Samran Pinnacle
description: An unmarked offshore pinnacle between Koh Tao and Sail Rock in open ocean. Strong currents bring large pelagic species including schools of hundreds of chevron barracuda. No buoy line — captains find it by GPS.

name: Lighthouse Bay
description: A sheltered bay on Koh Tao's northeast tip named after the lighthouse on the headland. Packed with toadstool soft corals, barrel sponges, and anemone colonies. Occasional sea turtle and blacktip reef shark visits.

name: Buddha Point
description: A hidden gem off the coast of Chalok Baan Kao, also known as Hin Taa Toh or Buddha Rock. A quiet, peaceful shallow reef at the edge of the bay near Freedom Beach with good visibility averaging 20 metres.

name: Chalok Bay
description: One of the most beginner-friendly dive sites on Koh Tao in the south coast's third-largest bay. Almost no current, with over 30,000 coral fragments planted as part of a marine conservation project.
